rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient is afebrile on a self-measured thermometer, well-perfused, with no infective focus, no marrow-failure signs, and a chronic stable mild neutropenia carrying no increased severe-infection risk; the acute symptoms are an anxiety response to a misinterpreted pathology alert. The appropriate disposition is a routine GP review within a week for a repeat full blood count and formal reassurance, paired with conservative red-flag safety-netting. Because telehealth cannot measure temperature/examine/obtain a blood count, the plan hinges on eliciting an objective temperature and giving clear escalation triggers. This is not an emergency; over-triaging a well, afebrile patient to ED would cause an unnecessary surge and reinforce health anxiety — the source note explicitly framed the assessment as preventing an inappropriate ED presentation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A true measured temperature of 38.0 degrees Celsius or higher
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department promptly if a fever develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever in a neutropenic patient is febrile neutropenia until proven otherwise and needs urgent assessment and often empirical IV antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your temperature reaches 38 degrees or higher, go to the nearest emergency department and tell them you have a low white-cell count — don't wait for your GP.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Uncontrollable shaking / rigors with teeth chattering
escalation_edges.rationale: True rigors suggest bacteraemia and, with neutropenia, warrant emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start shaking so hard you can't control it, with your teeth chattering, that's different from feeling shivery — go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New unexplained bruising, pinpoint red skin spots, bleeding gums, mouth ulcers or bone pain
escalation_edges.rationale: These suggest possible marrow failure / acute leukaemia and need urgent haematological assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you notice new bruises, tiny red spots on your skin, bleeding gums, mouth ulcers or bone pain, get assessed urgently at an emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling suddenly and profoundly unwell, faint/dizzy on standing, or a productive cough with a high fever
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests evolving sepsis or haemodynamic compromise rather than anxiety and needs emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel suddenly very unwell, dizzy when you stand, or develop a productive cough with a high fever, go straight to the emergency department, or call 000 if you feel profoundly unwell.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Anxiety or distress that keeps escalating and will not settle, or any thoughts of self-harm
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within 24-48 hours; use a crisis line immediately if any thoughts of self-harm
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent or worsening distress warrants prompt GP mental-health support; any self-harm ideation requires immediate crisis support.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the worry keeps getting worse and won't settle, see your GP in the next day or two for support. If you ever have thoughts of harming yourself, contact a crisis line or emergency services right away.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lowers the threshold to act on any TRUE fever — a measured temperature of 38 degrees or above warrants urgent ED assessment sooner than it would in someone with a normal count.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse present and under secondary stress from the patient's health anxiety
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Involve the spouse in the safety-net plan and give a clear, written red-flag list so anxiety does not distort recall and to reduce repeated after-hours contacts.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: See your GP within the next week to repeat the blood test and go through the result. In the meantime, if you feel hot or shivery, take your temperature with a thermometer. Go straight to an emergency department if your temperature reaches 38 degrees Celsius or higher, if you get uncontrollable shaking (rigors), or if you notice new bruising, tiny red skin spots, bleeding gums or mouth ulcers.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a routine appointment with your usual GP practice.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If a red-flag symptom develops after hours, go to the nearest emergency department, or call 000 if you feel profoundly unwell — don't wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring the pathology result and note the timeline of symptoms; ask the GP to compare the neutrophil count with any previous results to confirm it is stable.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismisses the presentation as pure anxiety/self-care without obtaining an objective temperature, screening for febrile-neutropenia and marrow-failure red flags, or arranging FBC follow-up.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An early febrile neutropenic sepsis or an acute leukaemic presentation is missed; the patient deteriorates at home believing they were reassured.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Provides reassurance but omits explicit emergency-department safety-netting for a true fever, rigors or new bleeding.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sends the afebrile, well-perfused patient to ED or calls an ambulance, causing an unnecessary ED surge and reinforcing the patient's health anxiety.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot measure temperature, examine for lymphadenopathy/petechiae, or obtain a blood count, so a covert early febrile neutropenia cannot be fully excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Elicit an objective home temperature, screen thoroughly for red flags, give proportionate reassurance, arrange prompt GP/FBC follow-up, and safety-net conservatively — escalate on any true fever or marrow-failure sign.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either declaring with false certainty that there is definitely no infection, or over-reacting by sending a well, afebrile patient to the emergency department.
